perturbation.original_text: 4. The obligations of confidentiality and non -use shall be in effect for a period of three (3) year following disclosure, and shall be binding upon the parties’ heirs, successors in interest, or assigns. Upon request, each party shall promptly return all documents or other tangible property containing Confidential Information furnished by the other or shall destroy any Confidential Information in tangible form in its possession.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The obligations of confidentiality and non-use shall be in effect for a period of three (3) years following disclosure for trade secrets only, and one (1) year for prototypes and formulas. These obligations shall be binding upon the parties’ heirs, successors in interest, or assigns. Upon request, each party shall promptly return all documents or other tangible property containing Confidential Information furnished by the other or shall destroy any Confidential Information in tangible form in its possession.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces different confidentiality periods depending on the type of confidential information. It now states that trade secrets are protected for three years, while prototypes and formulas are only protected for one year, creating an in-text contradiction of how Confidential Information should be treated and protected, since it makes no mention that there are different protection times in section 1, and would lead to confusion and ambiguity as to when the obligations of confidentiality ends.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. A party to this Agreement receiving Confidential Information “Recipient” agrees to retain such Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose it to any third party or use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than the purposes set forth in this Agreement, except for internal reporting. “Confidential Information” shall mean information related to the development of natural ingredients products including only trade secrets, prototypes, and formulas, whether written or oral. The Recipient will communicate such information only to such employees and contractors or agents having a need to know such information, and only to such employees and contractors or agents having written agreements with the Recipient obligating them to keep such information confidential .
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 4 to include different confidentiality periods for different types of 'Confidential Information' (3 years for trade secrets, 1 year for prototypes and formulas). This contradicts Section 1, which defines 'Confidential Information' as including trade secrets, prototypes, and formulas without specifying different protection periods. This creates a contradiction because Section 1 implies a uniform treatment of all confidential information, while the changed Section 4 introduces a differentiated treatment, leading to ambiguity about the actual confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The parties agre e no disclosure of information including or relating to such intellectual property sha ll be deemed a transfer or assignment of such intellectual property, nor shall any such disclosure of information be deemed a grant or waiver of any rights or privileges with respect to such intellectual property under applicable law, including but not limited to the right to prevent infringement by the unauthorized use, publication, or disclosure of such intellectual property.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The parties agree no disclosure of information including or relating to trade secrets shall be deemed a transfer or assignment of such intellectual property, nor shall any such disclosure of information be deemed a grant or waiver of any rights or privileges with respect to such intellectual property under applicable law, including but not limited to the right to prevent infringement by the unauthorized use, publication, or disclosure of such intellectual property. However, the disclosure of prototypes and formulas will be deemed a transfer or assignment of intellectual property.
perturbation.explanation: This section creates an in-text contradiction regarding the transfer of intellectual property rights. Initially, no disclosure of information is deemed a transfer. The change adds that disclosure of prototypes and formulas will be deemed a transfer. This conflicts with the fact that prototypes and formulas are considered Confidential Information under the definition of Confidential Information. It creates ambiguity about when a disclosure of Confidential Information relating to intellectual property does or doesn't constitute a transfer of rights.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. A party to this Agreement receiving Confidential Information “Recipient” agrees to retain such Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose it to any third party or use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than the purposes set forth in this Agreement, except for internal reporting. “Confidential Information” shall mean information related to the development of natural ingredients products including only trade secrets, prototypes, and formulas, whether written or oral. The Recipient will communicate such information only to such employees and contractors or agents who have a need to know such information, and only to such employees and contractors or agents having written agreements with the Recipient obligating them to keep such information confidential .
perturbation.justification: The original text states that no disclosure of information is deemed a transfer of intellectual property. The changed text adds an exception: disclosure of prototypes and formulas is deemed a transfer of intellectual property. The contradicted text defines 'Confidential Information' to include prototypes and formulas. This creates an in-text contradiction because the changed text says that disclosing prototypes and formulas leads to a transfer of IP, but the contradicted text requires that these same items are treated as Confidential Information, which should not be disclosed. The change creates a direct conflict regarding whether disclosure of prototypes and formulas leads to a transfer of IP rights, or whether it should be kept confidential.

perturbation.original_text: 8. This agreement shall be binding on the parties hereto and their respective subsidiaries, affiliates, divisions, officers, directors, agents, employees, successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This agreement shall be binding on the parties hereto, except for the clause pertaining to prototypes and formulas, and their respective subsidiaries, affiliates, divisions, officers, directors, agents, employees, successors and assigns.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an ambiguity and contradiction. While the clause is binding for the parties, specifically excludes the prototypes and formulas. It makes it unclear if the disclosure of prototypes or formulas constitutes a violation of this agreement. The contradiction directly affects the enforceability of the agreement regarding the confidential treatment of prototypes and formulas.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. A party to this Agreement receiving Confidential Information “Recipient” agrees to retain such Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose it to any third party or use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than the purposes set forth in this Agreement, except for internal reporting. “Confidential Information” shall mean information related to the development of natural ingredients products including only trade secrets, prototypes, and formulas, whether written or oral. The Recipient will communicate such information only to such employees and contractors or agents who have a need to know such information, and only to such employees and contractors or agents having written agreements with the Recipient obligating them to keep such information confidential .
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 8 explicitly excludes 'the clause pertaining to prototypes and formulas' from being binding on the parties. However, the contradicted text in section 1 defines 'Confidential Information' as including 'prototypes, and formulas' and requires the Recipient to keep such information confidential. This creates a direct contradiction as the changed text suggests that the confidential treatment of prototypes and formulas is not necessarily binding, while the contradicted text mandates it.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, during the course of such discussions, the parties may disclose to each other certain Confidential Information (as defin ed below);
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, during the course of such discussions, the parties may disclose to each other certain Confidential Information (as defined below); however, the definition of Confidential Information excludes all financial information of INaturals.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction in the 'WHEREAS' clauses. It states that parties will disclose Confidential Information as defined below, BUT financial information is not Confidential, creating a conflict because it implies that financial information might still be disclosed under the umbrella of Confidential Information, but the added clause excludes it. It is also contradictory to the definition of Confidential Information in section 1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. A party to this Agreement receiving Confidential Information “Recipient” agrees to retain such Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose it to any third party or use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than the purposes set forth in this Agreement, except for internal reporting. “Confidential Information” shall mean information related to the development of natural ingredients products including only trade secrets, prototypes, and formulas, whether written or oral. The Recipient will communicate such information only to such employees and contractors or agents who have a need to know such information, and only to such employees and contractors or agents having written agreements with the Recipient obligating them to keep such information confidential .
perturbation.justification: The original text states that parties may disclose Confidential Information. The changed text adds an exception, stating that financial information of INaturals is excluded from the definition of Confidential Information. However, section 1 defines "Confidential Information" to include information related to the development of natural ingredients products including only trade secrets, prototypes, and formulas, whether written or oral. This contradicts the changed text because it implies that while other confidential information may be disclosed, financial information is not included, thereby contradicting the comprehensive definition given in Section 1. The change creates a direct conflict by excluding financial information when Section 1's definition doesn't explicitly do so, satisfying the Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction definition.
